**Background Sources: History, Understanding, and Impact of Sycophancy**

**1\. Sharma, M., Tong, M., Korbak, T., Duvenaud, D., Askell, A., Bowman, S. R., Cheng, N., Durmus, E., Hatfield-Dodds, Z., Johnston, S. R., Kravec, S., Maxwell, T., McCandlish, S., Ndousse, K., Rausch, O., Schiefer, N., Yan, D., Zhang, M., & Perez, E. (2023, October 20). *Towards understanding sycophancy in language models*. arXiv.Org. https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.13548** 

Produced by researchers at Anthropic, this text offers a baseline methodology for understanding model alignment and human-feedback training. The authors argue that reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), or using positive/negative responses to generated responses in production, inadvertently trains models to prioritize user agreement over factual accuracy, noting that "both humans and preference models prefer convincingly-written sycophantic responses over correct ones a non-negligible fraction of the time." By testing models against varied prompts, the study demonstrates that sycophancy is a systemic flaw requiring structural mitigation rather than prompt engineering

**2\. Clegg, K.-A. (2025). Shoggoths, sycophancy, psychosis, oh my: Rethinking large language model use and safety. *Journal of Medical Internet Research*, *27*, e87367–e87367. https://doi.org/10.2196/87367** 

Published in a peer-reviewed medical journal (Journal of Medical Internet Research), this article examines the psychological impacts of prolonged, agreeable AI interactions. The author explores how syccophancy in large language models acts as an echo chamber for vulnerable users, warning that "LLMs may be contributing to the maintenance, reinforcement, or amplification of paranoid, false, or delusional beliefs." The text argues that the anthropomorphic and validating design of modern chatbots requires strict behavioral safeguards to prevent technology-induced psychological destabilization. Impact-focused, usable for introduction, answers “So What? Who Cares?” 

**3\. Malmqvist, L. (2024, November 22). *Sycophancy in large language models: Causes and mitigations*. arXiv.Org. https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.15287** 

This paper demonstrates a technical survey of model agreeability. Malmqvist argues that sycophancy stems from three sources: flattery-rich pre-training data, user-agreement reward mechanisms, and ineffective mitigation. Malmsvist proposes that these origins imply sycophancy may be more resistant to mitigation than other LLM operational issues. The text provides a framework for sycophancy mitigation, stating: “Promising mitigation strategies have emerged, with techniques like contrastive decoding, activation steering, and multi-agent approaches showing particular potential”; however, most proposed modifications from this source include mitigation strategies at the model training level. Could be a nice fallback in case all of our analytics come up short\!

**Methods: Papers with similar methodologies that we can borrow from**

**4\. Wei, J., Huang, D., Lu, Y., Zhou, D., & Le, Q. V. (2023, August 7). *Simple synthetic data reduces sycophancy in large language models*. arXiv.Org. https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.03958** 

Authored by researchers at Google DeepMind, this paper provides evidence on synthetic data interventions for AI alignment. The authors argue that scaling up models increases sycophantic behavior, but introducing targeted synthetic data can teach the system that "a statement's truthfulness is independent of a given user's opinion." They found that a lightweight fine-tuning step that significantly reduces a model's tendency to parrot incorrect user views.. **This seems exactly in line with our proposed intervention strategy\!** Even has some sample code at [https://github.com/google/sycophancy-intervention](https://github.com/google/sycophancy-intervention) 

**5\. Perez, E., Ringer, S., Lukosiute, K., Nguyen, K., Chen, E., Heiner, S., Pettit, C., Olsson, C., Kundu, S., Kadavath, S., Jones, A., Chen, A., Mann, B., Israel, B., Seethor, B., McKinnon, C., Olah, C., Yan, D., Amodei, D., … Kaplan, J. (2023). *Discovering language model behaviors with model-written evaluations*. ACL Anthology. [https://aclanthology.org/2023.findings-acl.847/](https://aclanthology.org/2023.findings-acl.847/)** 

Written by a team of AI safety researchers and published in the Association for Computational Linguistics, this study offers a general framework for evaluating LLM behaviors, offering several methodologies for different experiment types. The authors showcase that AI systems can be used to generate automated evaluations, comparing LM labels and evaluations to crowd-sourced evaluations, showing similar accuracy/outputs in validation. They also used LMs to generate synthetic data with semantic similarity (borrowing methods from [Generating Datasets with Pretrained Language Models](https://aclanthology.org/2021.emnlp-main.555/))

**6\. Yeung, J. A., Dalmasso, J., Foschini, L., Dobson, R. J., & Kraljevic, Z. (2025, September 13). *The psychogenic machine: simulating AI psychosis, delusion reinforcement and harm enablement in large language models*. arXiv.Org. [https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.10970](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.10970)** 

Led by clinical and neurological researchers, this recent study provides a concrete evaluation metric for model delusion that we can slightly tweak for our sycophancy evaluation. The paper “introduces psychosis-bench, a novel benchmark designed to systematically evaluate the psychogenicity of LLMs.” By simulating multi-turn conversations based on clinical psychosis reports, the text shows how and when models frequently fail to deploy safety interventions. We could use a similar 12 (or general multi)-turn framework, monitoring how model sycophancy likelihood develops as the context window fills.

![][image1]

**7\. Anonymous. (2025). *Truth decay: Quantifying multi-turn sycophancy in language models*. OpenReview. [https://openreview.net/forum?id=GHUh9O5Im8](https://openreview.net/forum?id=GHUh9O5Im8)** 

**Currently in double blind review.** This paper provides general methodologies for assessing AI behavior over extended dialogues. The metric “Truth Decay” is designed to track sycophancy rate as conversations continue (borrowing from the 12-turn methodology of  **Au Yeung, J., et al).** This metric can absolutely be used in our model evaluations, and they use a similar methodology to our proposed framework. Our paper can offer new insights, since this study was done on Claude Haiku, GPT 4.1, and an outdated Llama model.

**Semantic Analysis: Foundational NLP texts to use for analytics/discussion**

**8\. Reimers, N., & Gurevych, I. (2019, August 27). *Sentence-BERT: Sentence Embeddings using Siamese BERT-Networks*. arXiv.Org. https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.10084** 

This paper introduces SBERT, which allows for efficient calculation of semantic similarity via cosine similarity of sentence embeddings. The authors argue that traditional BERT is too slow for large-scale comparisons, whereas SBERT "maintains high accuracy while drastically reducing computational overhead." This provides the mathematical basis for our plan to cluster prompts and responses semantically. Methodology: sentence embed prompt/response using SBERT, evaluate sycophancy using frameworks (from sources 6 and 7), then examine for semantic similarity among failure/success cases. Also could be used to track for semantic differences when offered a system prompt.

**9\. Zhang, T., Kishore, V., Wu, F., Weinberger, K. Q., & Artzi, Y. (2019, April 21). *BERTScore: Evaluating text generation with BERT*. arXiv.Org. https://arxiv.org/abs/1904.09675** 

This paper proposes BERTScore, a metric that evaluates the similarity of generated text by comparing token embeddings rather than exact word matches. The authors state that this method "more robustly captures semantic equivalence" than traditional metrics like BLEU or ROUGE. This is critical for our project to determine if an AI's response is semantically aligned with a Reddit "ground truth" even when using different vocabulary, and gives us an option that isn’t just LM-as-evaluator.

**10\. Gao, T., Yao, X., & Chen, D. (2021, April 18). *Simcse: Simple contrastive learning of sentence embeddings*. arXiv.Org. https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.08821** 

The authors present SimCSE, a contrastive learning framework that significantly improves the quality of sentence embeddings for similarity tasks. They argue that using dropout as "noise" during training helps the model better distinguish between semantically similar and dissimilar inputs. This source will help us refine our analytic NLP approach when categorizing "sycophancy-triggering" prompt variations, though it is unlikely that we’ll use this to train our own NLP models. May be moved to background information / methodological defense
